title: Writing - Part 1: Renewable Energy
description: Your class has attended a debate on the most effective strategies governments should adopt to accelerate the transition to renewable energy sources.

content.question: Write an essay discussing two of the strategies in your notes. You should explain which strategy is most likely to produce lasting results, giving reasons for your opinion.

opinions: Making solar panels and electric cars affordable is the fastest way to drive adoption.
If polluting is expensive enough, companies will find cleaner alternatives themselves.
People won't change unless they understand why it matters.

structure.introduction: Introduce the urgency of the energy transition and the range of policy options available.
structure.bodyParagraph1: Evaluate the effectiveness of subsidising green technologies.
structure.bodyParagraph2: Examine the merits and limitations of carbon taxation.
structure.conclusion: Conclude by arguing which strategy offers the most sustainable path to a green economy.

content.modelAnswer:
The transition away from fossil fuels is no longer simply an environmental aspiration—it is an economic and political imperative. Governments worldwide are grappling with how to accelerate this shift, and two of the most discussed policy tools are the subsidisation of green technologies and the imposition of carbon taxes. This essay will evaluate the potential of each approach.

Subsidies for renewable energy sources and clean technologies have already demonstrated their effectiveness in numerous countries. By lowering the cost of solar panels, wind turbines, and electric vehicles, governments can make sustainable options accessible to ordinary consumers rather than only to the wealthy. This demand-side stimulus creates a virtuous cycle: greater adoption drives economies of scale, which in turn reduces prices further and reduces dependence on state support over time. The primary drawback is the significant fiscal cost, which must be financed through taxation or public debt.

Carbon taxation, by contrast, operates on the principle that polluters should bear the true cost of their environmental impact. By making fossil fuel use progressively more expensive, governments create a powerful financial incentive for businesses and individuals to innovate and adapt. However, without careful design, such taxes can disproportionately burden lower-income households, undermining public support and political viability.

In conclusion, I believe that subsidising green technology offers the more dependable route to lasting change. While carbon taxes can be effective, their social and political risks are considerable. Direct investment in clean alternatives not only drives adoption but builds the industrial infrastructure needed to sustain a genuinely green economy in the long term.
